text:
Olympian Rebecca Cheptegei, who died earlier this month after her partner set her on fire, has been buried in Uganda.

Dickson Ndiema Marangach, 32, assaulted Ms Cheptegei in her home in Endebess near the Ugandan border on 1 September.

The 33-year-old, who debuted this summer in the women's marathon at the Paris Olympics, died as a result of severe burns.

The brutal assault shocked the East African region and prompted a global outpouring of tributes, with activists condemning another act of gender-based violence.

Ms Cheptegei's family say the couple had argued over ownership of the property where she lived with her sister and daughters. Her attacker later died from injuries sustained in the assault.

It is the latest incident of gender-based violence in Kenya.

Rain fell as the athlete's body crossed the Kenya-Uganda border late yesterday afternoon ahead of the funeral in the family's village of Bukwo, some 380km northeast of Uganda's capital Kampala.

"We are extremely saddened," said her estranged husband Simon Ayeko, with whom she had two daughters.

"As a father it has been very difficult," he said, explaining he had not been able to break the news to their children.

"Slowly we will tell them the truth."

Ms Cheptegei, a sergeant in the Uganda Peoples' Defence Forces, was buried with military honours, Beatrice Ayikoru, Uganda's Olympics Committee secretary general and part of the burial organising committee, said.

Scores of athletes travelled to the small village to attend.

"She greatly contributed to the promotion of athletics until her last days," coach Alex Malinga, who trained her as a teenager, said.

Local media say Ms Cheptegei's daughters witnessed the attack. Police said Marangach snuck into her home while she was at church with her children.

"I think at that time, their relationship had become sour," Ms Cheptegei's brother-in-law, Moses Kipsiro, said.

"I didn't know then something was wrong," said Mr Kipsiro, who previously trained with Ms Cheptegei and also hails from Bukwo.

The vicious assault has thrown yet another spotlight on what activists have called a femicide epidemic.Kenya reported 725 femicide cases in 2022 alone, according to the latest UN figures.

A report the following year by Kenya's National Bureau of Statistics found 34 percent of women had experienced physical violence since the age of 15.

At least two other athletes, Agnes Tirop and Damaris Mutua, have lost their lives in domestic violence incidents since 2021.

Source:AFP

title: Uganda holds funeral for murdered Olympian Cheptegei
description: Olympian Rebecca Cheptegei, who died earlier this month after her partner set her on fire, has been buried in Uganda.
